reference: GATE CS — Linear Algebra / Matrices and Systems

map.condition: Solving Ax = b, check if unique / infinite / no solution
Eigenvalues of a triangular or diagonal matrix
det after row operations or products

formulas.name: Determinant of a 2×2 Matrix
formulas.plain: det([a,b;c,d]) = a*d - b*c
formulas.whenToUse: Direct computation of a 2×2 determinant.
formulas.trap: Students often swap the minus sign: remember it is ad − bc, not bc − ad.

formulas.name: Determinant of a Product
formulas.plain: det(A*B) = det(A) * det(B)
formulas.whenToUse: When the determinant of a matrix product needs to be evaluated without expanding the product.

terms.means: square matrices of the same order

formulas.trap: This only holds for square matrices of the same size; it does NOT extend to rectangular matrices.

formulas.name: Determinant of a Transpose
formulas.plain: det(A^T) = det(A)
formulas.whenToUse: Simplifying determinant calculations when the transpose is easier to work with.
formulas.trap: Students confuse det(A^T) = det(A) with det(A^{-1}) = 1/det(A); these are different identities.

formulas.name: Determinant of an Inverse
formulas.plain: det(A^-1) = 1 / det(A)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the determinant of the inverse matrix without explicitly computing the inverse.

terms.means: inverse of matrix A; exists only when det(A) ≠ 0

formulas.trap: A is invertible if and only if det(A) ≠ 0; applying this formula when det(A) = 0 is undefined.

formulas.name: Determinant Under Scalar Multiplication
formulas.plain: det(k*A) = k^n * det(A)
formulas.whenToUse: When every entry of an n×n matrix is scaled by a constant k.
formulas.trap: A very common GATE error: forgetting the exponent n and writing det(kA) = k·det(A).

formulas.name: Effect of Row Operations on Determinant
formulas.latex: \text{Row swap} \Rightarrow \det \times (-1);\quad R_i \leftarrow kR_i \Rightarrow \det \times k;\quad R_i \leftarrow R_i + cR_j \Rightarrow \det \text{ unchanged}
formulas.plain: Row swap: det *= -1; Scale row by k: det *= k; Add multiple of another row: det unchanged
formulas.whenToUse: Tracking how Gaussian elimination or cofactor expansion changes the determinant.

terms.means: the i-th row of the matrix
scalar used to scale a row

formulas.trap: Only scaling a SINGLE row multiplies det by k; scaling the whole matrix multiplies det by k^n.

formulas.name: Inverse of a 2×2 Matrix
formulas.whenToUse: Quick closed-form inverse for a 2×2 matrix.
formulas.trap: The adjugate swaps the main diagonal entries and negates the off-diagonal; mixing up signs is the classic error.

formulas.name: Rank-Nullity Theorem
formulas.plain: rank(A) + nullity(A) = n  (number of columns)
formulas.whenToUse: Relating the dimension of the column space to the dimension of the null space of an m×n matrix.

terms.means: number of linearly independent columns (= row rank)
dimension of the null space of A

formulas.trap: n here is the number of COLUMNS, not rows; confusing with m (rows) gives wrong nullity.

formulas.name: Invertibility Condition
formulas.plain: A invertible <=> det(A) != 0 <=> rank(A) = n <=> nullity(A) = 0
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a system has a unique solution or whether an inverse exists.
formulas.trap: All four conditions are equivalent; GATE questions sometimes give one and ask you to infer another.

formulas.name: Characteristic Equation
formulas.plain: det(A - lambda*I) = 0
formulas.whenToUse: Finding eigenvalues of any square matrix.

terms.means: identity matrix of same order as A

formulas.trap: det(A - λI) = 0, NOT det(λI - A) = 0 for sign consistency (both give same roots, but sign of coefficients changes).

formulas.name: Trace and Determinant via Eigenvalues
formulas.plain: trace(A) = sum of eigenvalues;  det(A) = product of eigenvalues
formulas.whenToUse: Quick check or computation of trace/det from known eigenvalues, or to find a missing eigenvalue.

terms.means: the i-th eigenvalue of A (with multiplicity)

formulas.trap: These identities count eigenvalues WITH algebraic multiplicity; missing a repeated eigenvalue is a common mistake.

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of Triangular / Diagonal Matrices
formulas.plain: For triangular (or diagonal) A, eigenvalues are the diagonal entries a_ii
formulas.whenToUse: Reading off eigenvalues directly without solving the characteristic polynomial.
formulas.trap: This applies to triangular matrices ONLY; off-diagonal entries do not affect eigenvalues but DO affect eigenvectors.

formulas.name: Cayley-Hamilton Theorem
formulas.plain: Every square matrix satisfies its own characteristic polynomial: p(A) = 0
formulas.whenToUse: Computing powers of a matrix or expressing A^k in terms of lower powers.

terms.means: the polynomial evaluated with the matrix A substituted for λ

formulas.trap: p(A) = 0 means the MATRIX polynomial is zero, not just the scalar determinant; students sometimes misapply this to vectors.

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of Matrix Powers and Functions
formulas.plain: If lambda is eigenvalue of A with eigenvector x, then lambda^k is eigenvalue of A^k with same x
formulas.whenToUse: Determining eigenvalues of A^k, A^{-1}, or polynomial functions of A without recomputing.
formulas.trap: The eigenvector stays the SAME; only the eigenvalue changes. Also, A^{-1} requires λ ≠ 0.

formulas.name: Rouché–Capelli (Consistency) Theorem
formulas.plain: Ax = b has a solution iff rank(A) = rank([A|b])
formulas.whenToUse: Determining whether a system of linear equations has any solution at all.

terms.means: augmented matrix formed by appending b as a column to A

formulas.trap: Consistency only tells you IF a solution exists; you still need to compare rank vs n for uniqueness.

formulas.name: Number of Solutions of Ax = b
formulas.plain: rank(A) != rank([A|b]) => no solution; rank(A)=rank([A|b])=n => unique; rank(A)=rank([A|b])<n => infinite
formulas.whenToUse: The definitive check for how many solutions Ax = b has.
formulas.trap: The three cases are mutually exclusive; do not confuse 'infinite solutions' with 'no solution' — both have rank(A) < n but differ in augmented rank.

formulas.name: Homogeneous System Ax = 0
formulas.latex: Ax = 0 \text{ has a non-trivial solution} \iff \text{rank}(A) < n \iff \det(A) = 0
formulas.plain: Ax = 0 has non-trivial solution iff rank(A) < n  (i.e., det(A) = 0 for square A)
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a homogeneous system has solutions beyond the trivial x = 0.
formulas.trap: The trivial solution x = 0 ALWAYS exists; the question is whether non-trivial solutions exist.

formulas.name: Cramer's Rule
formulas.plain: x_i = det(A_i) / det(A), where A_i is A with column i replaced by b
formulas.whenToUse: Solving a small (2×2 or 3×3) system with a unique solution using determinants.

terms.means: matrix A with the i-th column replaced by the right-hand side vector b

formulas.trap: Cramer's rule requires det(A) ≠ 0 (unique solution); it does NOT work for singular or overdetermined systems.

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of Symmetric Matrices
formulas.latex: A = A^T \implies \lambda_i \in \mathbb{R} \text{ and eigenvectors for distinct } \lambda \text{ are orthogonal}
formulas.plain: Symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues
formulas.whenToUse: Any question involving a real symmetric matrix and its spectral properties.
formulas.trap: Orthogonality is guaranteed only for DISTINCT eigenvalues; repeated eigenvalues need explicit orthogonalization.

formulas.name: Idempotent Matrix Properties
formulas.plain: A^2 = A => eigenvalues are 0 or 1; rank(A) = trace(A)
formulas.whenToUse: Questions involving projection matrices or any matrix satisfying A² = A.

terms.means: trace of A = sum of diagonal entries

formulas.trap: rank(A) = tr(A) is specific to idempotent matrices; do NOT apply this identity to arbitrary matrices.

formulas.name: Orthogonal Matrix Properties
formulas.plain: Orthogonal matrix: A^T = A^-1, det(A) = +1 or -1
formulas.whenToUse: Questions on rotation/reflection matrices or matrices with orthonormal columns.

terms.means: defining property of an orthogonal matrix

formulas.trap: det(A) = ±1 (not just +1); a reflection also has orthogonal matrix form but det = −1.

formulas.name: Nilpotent Matrix Properties
formulas.latex: A^k = 0 \text{ for some } k \implies \lambda_i = 0 \text{ for all } i
formulas.plain: Nilpotent matrix (A^k = 0): all eigenvalues are 0
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying eigenvalues or powers of a nilpotent matrix.

terms.means: smallest positive integer such that A^k = 0, called the index of nilpotency

formulas.trap: All eigenvalues being 0 does NOT mean the matrix itself is 0 (e.g., a strictly upper-triangular matrix).

formulas.name: Adjugate (Adjoint) and Inverse
formulas.plain: A^-1 = adj(A) / det(A);  A * adj(A) = det(A) * I
formulas.whenToUse: Computing the inverse using cofactors, or questions about adj(A).

terms.means: transpose of the cofactor matrix of A

formulas.trap: adj(A) is the TRANSPOSE of the cofactor matrix; students often forget to transpose after computing cofactors.

formulas.name: Matrix Power via Diagonalization
formulas.plain: If A = P*D*P^-1 then A^k = P * diag(lambda_i^k) * P^-1
formulas.whenToUse: Computing large powers of a diagonalizable matrix efficiently.
formulas.trap: A must be diagonalizable (n linearly independent eigenvectors); defective matrices require Jordan form instead.
